coredata.dc:title: Inner filter effect mediated fluorescent sensing of rutin based on amino acid-derived novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots

coredata.dc:description:
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were constructed through a hydrothermal synthesis employing l-cysteine and d-aspartic acid as precursors in this study. A novel scheme has been proposed for synthesizing N,S-CDs utilizing two amino acids, aiming to detect rutin. The N,S-CDs exhibit excellent luminescence performance as fluorescent sensors with a dimension of 2.5 ± 0.14 nm and excitation/emission wavelengths of 350/429 nm. The rutin quenching mechanisms in N,S-CDs was verified as the inner filter effect (IFE) by spectral analysis, fluorescence lifetime detection, and Parker equation calculation. The system features with N,S-CDs and rutin as the IFE fluorophore and IFE absorber. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of N,S-CDs presented a good linear correlation with rutin concentration in the 20–500 μmol·L-1 range based on (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0014C + 0.0152 (R
2 = 0.9941) with the detection limit of 8.05 μmol·L-1. This method accurately detects the rutin concentration in samples of human urine and serum. The technique developed in this research boasts a wide linear range, fast response time, a wide pH range, excellent stability, and simplicity. It holds a promising potential for the application in the medicine and pharmacy fields.

head.ce:title: Inner filter effect mediated fluorescent sensing of rutin based on amino acid-derived novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were constructed through a hydrothermal synthesis employing l-cysteine and d-aspartic acid as precursors in this study. A novel scheme has been proposed for synthesizing N,S-CDs utilizing two amino acids, aiming to detect rutin. The N,S-CDs exhibit excellent luminescence performance as fluorescent sensors with a dimension of 2.5 ± 0.14 nm and excitation/emission wavelengths of 350/429 nm. The rutin quenching mechanisms in N,S-CDs was verified as the inner filter effect (IFE) by spectral analysis, fluorescence lifetime detection, and Parker equation calculation. The system features with N,S-CDs and rutin as the IFE fluorophore and IFE absorber. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of N,S-CDs presented a good linear correlation with rutin concentration in the 20–500 μmol·L-1 range based on (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0014C + 0.0152 (R
2 = 0.9941) with the detection limit of 8.05 μmol·L-1. This method accurately detects the rutin concentration in samples of human urine and serum. The technique developed in this research boasts a wide linear range, fast response time, a wide pH range, excellent stability, and simplicity. It holds a promising potential for the application in the medicine and pharmacy fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rutin commonly referred to as vitamin P, rutoside, or sophorin, chemically described as 3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone-3-rutinoside [1], is categorized as a flavonol glycoside. Fig. 1 shows its chemical structure. Rutin is widely found in medicinal foods such as Sophora japonica, buckwheat, tea, wolfberry, jujube and Astragali Radix [2], and is also present in high levels in vegetables and fruits such as lemons, citrus, grapes, tomatoes, asparagus and eggplant [3]. Extensive pharmacological studies have focused on its potential benefits for preventing and treating various diseases, including anticancer [4], cardioprotective [5], anti-inflammatory, antioxidant [6], antihyperglycemic [7], neuroprotective [8], antidepressant [9], and hypolipidemic properties [10]. Based on these prominent pharmacological activities and excellent physiological properties, rutin has attracted considerable attention in the fields of pharmaceutical preparations, nutraceutical products, clinical drugs, food, cosmetics and so on. As an additive, it is used in more than 130 pharmaceutical preparations [11]. Meanwhile, it is also an important component of many health care products as a natural health protector [12]. In addition, rutin acts as an antioxidant in many foods and pharmaceuticals [13]. There are many ways for people to intake rutin in daily life. However, excessive intake of rutin will result in a number of serious side effects, such as blurred vision, gastrointestinal discomfort, skin problems, excessive CNS stimulation, and liver damage [14–18]. Consequently, it is highly critical to develop a easy-to-use, affordable, rapid, and sensitive sensor for monitoring of rutin content in biological samples. Furthermore, rutin has low bioavailability due to its poor water solubility, which has aroused interest in pharmaceutical research. Various novel drug-delivery methods of rutin have been studied in order to solve this problem [19,20]. And pharmacokinetics is also particularly interested in the metabolism of rutin [12,21]. Therefore, in this study, the method of detecting rutin in serum and urine is not only useful in pharmaceutical analysis, but also helpful for the study of pharmacology and pharmacokinetics.

Numerous techniques were available for analyzing rutin to date, such as electrochemical detection [22,23], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [24,25], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [26], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [27,28] and chemiluminescence (CL) [29,30]. However, implementing the above methods requires expensive instruments, complex sample pretreatment, specialized personnel training, long testing times and complicated testing procedures. These drawbacks limit their applicability and make them unsuitable for large-scale sample testing. Notably, the fluorescence detection method offers relatively inexpensive instrumentation, rapid detection, high sensitivity, portable use and simple pre-processing, making it highly suitable for practical use. The design of fluorescence sensors is crucial in fluorescence detection technology.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel zero-dimensional carbon nanoluminescent materials, generally spherical carbon particles with a dimension of no more than 10 nm, consisting of surface functional groups combined with carbon cores, at the crossroads of luminescent semiconductor quantum dots and carbon materials research. In recent times, they have garnered considerable interest because of their outstanding and distinctive characteristics [31,32]. Carbon dots prepared by inexpensive, environmentally friendly and simple synthetic routes can exhibit excellent photostability, extremely low cytotoxicity, very high biocompatibility, excellent water solubility and easy surface modification [32,33]. The above advantages make them widely used in multiple fields, containing fluorescence sensing [34,35], drug delivery [36,37], bioimaging [38,39], optoelectronic devices [40], photocatalysis [41] and anti-counterfeiting [42]. Carbon dots can be synthesized from various small organic molecules with excellent luminescent properties by hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis [43,44], microwave synthesis [45] and pyrolysis [46].

All life depends on amino acids. As the elementary unit of proteins, they play an irreplaceable role in maintaining normal life activities. Amino acids are involved in the synthesis of many enzymes, hormones and antibodies in the human body. Therefore, amino acids are an excellent green precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots. Amino acids are distinctive compounds characterized by carboxyl (-COOH) and amino (-NH2) groups. Amino groups may be retained on the surface of carbon dots in large quantities, which improves their biocompatibility [47]. At the same time, the presence of carboxyl groups will enhance water solubility. Under hydrothermal conditions, amino acids will dehydrate and cause carbonization [48]. Furthermore, incorporating non-metallic elements like nitrogen and sulfur into carbon dots proves to be an effective method for adjust their performance [49].

Nitrogen doping stands out as the most effective approach for altering the characteristics of fluorescence among carbon dots. Through nitrogen-doping, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) will increase significantly, the surface will be passivated, and the emission specifics may change. After sulfur-doping, optical and chemical stability will be enhanced, and QY will also increase [50]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that N,S-CDs showcase outstanding fluorescence properties [51–53]. The reasons for changes in performance upon heteroatom doping may be that they can produce new defect sites, additional active sites and electron pairs, thereby changing chemical activity of carbon dots [54].

As a common biological small molecule, amino acids can provide both N and S elements simultaneously. With inherent advantages, it stands as an excellent eco-friendly precursor for synthesizing N,S-CDs. All amino acids have carbon and nitrogen elements, and cysteine and methionine contain S elements. It inspired us to think that N,S-CDs can be theoretically obtained through mixing sulfur-containing and sulfur-free amino acids together. Zeng et al. [48] reported a method for preparing N,S-CDs by hydrothermal synthesis through mixing l-serine and l-cystine, successfully applying it to imaging mouse peritoneal macrophages. Kang et al. [50] synthesized N,S-CDs employing l-alanine, l-cysteine, and dl-malic acid, subsequently assessing their fluorescence properties and antibacterial activity.

The Inner Filter Effect (IFE) appears due to absorbers absorbing the excitation/emission light from fluorophores within the detection system [55]. In this system, the absorption spectrum of these absorbers coincides with excitation and/or emission bands of fluorophores, which is a prerequisite for the occurrence of IFE [56]. In this study, N,S-CDs served as the fluorophore, while rutin acted as the absorber. Designing fluorescent sensors based on IFE is an effective strategy. In contrast to other mechanisms, IFE-based detection does not necessitate the modification of CDs, intricate calculation processes, or covalent linking an absorber to a fluorophore. At the same time, its synthesis method is simple, the cost is low, and the flexibility is high.

In this paper, N,S-CDs were constructed by conducting a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. l-cysteine and d-aspartic acid acted as precursors, while rutin was detected by fluorescence spectroscopy (Scheme 1 ). Among them, carbon and nitrogen origin from d-aspartic acid, and l-cysteine supplies carbon, sulfur and nitrogen. Within a defined range, there exists a clear linear correlation of the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs with rutin concentration. On account of experimental results, significantly reduced N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity was attributed to the IFE which is the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The method established during this study has the benefits of high stability, environmentally friendly, a broad linear range, a wide pH range, and capable of detecting rutin with speed, ease, and accuracy. The method has been effectively employed to detect rutin in the samples of human urine and serum, suggesting promising prospects for its application.

First, l-cysteine (0.605 g, 5 mmol) and d-aspartic acid (0.665 g, 5 mmol) were dissolved in ultrapure water (20 mL) and sonicated for 10 min until fully dissolved. The prepared solution was later put into a 30 mL high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon). Following that, the autoclave was heated up to 200 °C, holding for 6 h in a oven. Once cooling down to room temperature, a 0.22 μm microporous membrane was used for filtration to gain a yellow reaction solution. N,S-CDs were stored directly in the solution. The yellow solution were kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator after being sealed in a container.

400 μL rutin solution (20–500 μM) with different concentrations was severally added into centrifuge tubes with 100 μL N,S-CDs solution, followed by adding 2.5 mL PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4). Through agitating for mixture, the system was allowed to stand for 1 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of different rutin concentrations was assessed using a 350 nm excitation wavelength. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate for reproducibility.

The established N,S-CDs fluorescence sensing analysis method was used to detect rutin in body fluid, including human urine and serum. These samples were processed for filtration with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and diluted 100 times with PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) for subsequent use. For the investigation of the recovery rate, diluted urine and serum samples were spiked with low, medium, as well as high concentrations of rutin solution. The spiked samples underwent analysis using identical procedures and conditions outlined previously for rutin detection. Human sera were from the Hospital of Jilin University (first affiliated) with approval from the ethics committee.

Fluorescence quantum yield refers to the ratio of the number of photons emitted by the fluorescence material after absorption to the number of photons absorbed by the excitation light [57]. It is an important index for measuring the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent substances and is also one of the characteristics used to evaluate the performance of luminescent materials. Quantum yield is divided into absolute quantum yield (Abs-QY) and relative quantum yield. The measurement of relative quantum yield can lead to experimental errors due to improper wavelength selection of standard materials. The advantage of absolute quantum yield is that it does not require comparison with standard material, resulting in small measurement errors, and stable value.

In this study, we used the steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000) equipped with an integrating sphere accessory to measure Abs-QY of N,S-CDs under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The blank control is phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS, pH = 7.4), which is solvent of N,S-CDs. In addition, the scatter range is from 337 to 361 nm, and the emission range is from 373.5 to 687 nm, when calculating Abs-QY by the method of integral. The absolute quantum yield can be calculated using the following formula:
(1)

represent the number of photons of the solvent (PBS, pH = 7.4) and N,S-CDs solution excited by the excitation light, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was deployed for analyzing the pattern and size of N,S-CDs constructed from l-cysteine and d-aspartic acid. In Fig. 2 a, the dispersion was observed to be satisfactory, with uniform particle size distribution and no observable aggregation of N,S-CDs. Fig. 2b displayed the histogram illustrating the distribution of particle sizes. The mean size was about 2.5 ± 0.14 nm with a distribution between 1.47 and 2.96 nm, based on a sample size of 54. The central peak was at 2θ = 20.6°, indicating the presence of the (311) carbon plane (Fig. 2d) [58].

The elemental composition, elemental content as well as surface functional groups of samples were determined through XPS. Four distinctive peaks at 163.08, 285.08, 400.08 and 531.08 eV were found in the XPS survey for N,S-CDs (Fig. 3 a), which separately belong to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s [71,72]. Furthermore, XPS analysis revealed the composition of N,S-CDs, comprising S, C, N, and O, with atomic percentages of 6.95 %, 64.13 %, 8.52 %, and 20.4 %, respectively (Fig. 3b). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3c) was matched into four absorption peaks. The two bands at 163.12 and 164.27 eV were considered as C–S–C at in S2p, which dominate. The remaining small fraction could be ascribed to –SO2 and –SO3 group (167.75 eV, 168.90 eV) [47,73–75]. This further confirms the presence of S doping in N,S-CDs. Three fitted bands at 284.80, 285.68 and 288.18 eV in C 1s (Fig. 3d) signified C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N, and CO bonds. Fig. 3e displayed the spectrum of N 1s resolved into fitting peaks at 399.68 eV and 401.27 eV, considering as pyrrole N and amino N [69,75,76], further indicating the successful incorporation of nitrogen within the structure of N,S-CDs. After fitting the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s, they were observed at 531.37 and 532.82 eV, demonstrating the presence of CO and C–O bonds (Fig. 3f) [71]. These XPS data were in good agreement with the FT-IR spectral analysis, verifying that the successful synthesis of the N,S-CDs. The two characterization results are mutually supportive, ensuring the accuracy of the conclusions.

The optical properties were evaluated through UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrophotometry, and results of N,S-CDs were revealed by the blue curve in Fig. 4 a. In the figure, a significant absorption band about 237 nm was observed, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of CC. On the other hand, the shoulder peak observed at 266 nm may be caused by the n-π∗ transition induced by CO. Additionally, as shown in the insets of Fig. 4a, N,S-CDs were yellow color under natural light and displayed bright blue fluorescence under exposure to ultraviolet light with 365 nm wavelength, indicating that N,S-CDs have good dispersibility and strong photoluminescence properties in solution. 429 and 350 nm were separately deemed as the maximum emission and excitation wavelength in N,S-CDs. This investigation revealed that the emission spectrum increased as the excitation wavelength ranged from 310 to 380 nm, suggesting wavelength-dependent excitation characteristics of the synthesized N,S-CDs (Fig. 4b). The N,S-CDs have the strongest emission at 429 nm under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm with a Stokes shift of 79 nm and the Abs-QY of N,S-CDs reached 4.96 %, illustrating that N,S-CDs possessed a favorable level of luminescent ability. These findings explain the prominent dispersion as well as optical properties of N,S-CDs sample.

To acknowledge an appropriate pH range for rutin detection, N,S-CDs and rutin were placed in solutions of pH 3.0–11.0 to identify the fluorescence intensity of the system. The fluorescence intensity remained relatively constant across the pH from 3.0 to 11.0 (Fig. S1a), indicating that the detection of rutin can be carried out in a wide pH range, which further broadens the application range of the fluorescent sensor. Table S2 summarized the pH range of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of rutin. By comparison, it can be found that N, S-CDs have a wider pH range than other fluorescent sensors in the detection of rutin. Based on the pH value of the actual biological samples, pH = 7.4 was deemed as the optimal threshold for rutin determination using N,S-CDs. To remove the interference of incubation times of N,S-CDs and rutin from the detection results, the association of incubation time with the fluorescence intensity was explored. As shown in Fig. S1b, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs decreased rapidly after the addition of rutin, and then kept nearly constant for up to 80 min, suggesting that N,S-CDs could rapidly detect rutin and increasing the incubation time had little impact on the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1b). Taking into account the practical detection scenario, the fluorescence intensity was detected 1 min after the addition of rutin. The fluorescence sensor developed in this experiment significantly improved the detection speed and reduced the time cost.

The stability of CDs is one of the important factors that determine its application range. The stability exposed to varying pH, radiation time, ionic strength, temperature, as well as storage time was examined for N,S-CDs in this work. Fig. 5 a suggested that the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs maintained relative stability under pH 3.0–11.0. The results demonstrated that the N,S-CDs had high pH stability in the broad pH range of 3.0–11.0. However, beyond this range, the fluorescence intensity would increase or decrease. The possible explanation is that the protonation and deprotonation states of the surface functional groups change significantly when N,S-CDs are in a too acidic or alkaline environment [77,78]. The same system under various NaCl solution concentrations was depicted in Fig. 5b. Even with the salt concentration reaching 1.0 mol·L-1, it showed minimal variation and stayed relatively constant. This indicates that N,S-CDs have a high salt resistance, which makes them suitable for component analysis in biological samples. In an effort to thoroughly explore the photostability of N,S-CDs, they were exposed to continuous irradiation within 365 nm ultraviolet light. The results revealed that following 100 min of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity remained consistently at around 86.6 %, which proved that N,S-CDs have a remarkable photobleaching resistance (see Fig. 5c). Furthermore, as indicated in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence intensity reduced as temperature rose, suggesting that elevated temperatures adversely impact its stability. In addition, N,S-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, and the fluorescence intensity was measured daily to observe the change of fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly within 30 days, which indicates that N,S-CDs have outstanding storage stability. However, after 30 days, the fluorescence intensity considerable decreased, indicating that the effective use period of the prepared N,S-CDs is about 1 month. Moreover, the same N,S-CDs were continuously scanned at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs continuously scanned under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm did not change significantly (Fig. 5f). According to the above results, N,S-CDs have excellent pH stability, salt tolerance, photobleaching resistance, and storage stability, which paves the way for the practical detection of rutin in the future and is suitable for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyzing performance of N,S-CDs on rutin
ce:section.ce:para:
The response of N,S-CDs to rutin was investigated under the optimized detection environment. As revealed in Fig. 6 b, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs for the emission peak at 429 nm steadily declined, with increasing rutin concentration. Fig. 6a showed that within the scope of 20–500 μmol·L-1, the consequence of (F0–F)/F0 had a linear association with rutin addition, described by (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0014C + 0.0152 (R
2 = 0.9941). In this formula, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in the absence of rutin and the system following the presence of rutin. The C defines rutin concentration. On basis of formula 3σ/k, the calculated detection limit was 8.05 μmol·L-1. This study proposed a method for synthesizing N,S-CDs using l-cysteine and d-aspartic acid as precursors and as a fluorescent sensor to detect rutin. As revealed in Table S1, this approach exhibited a wider linear range and quicker response time than other published methods. What's more, it confirmed advantages including outstanding storage stability, simple synthesis operation, and high convenience, making it suitable for effectively detecting rutin in practical applications.

Under normal circumstances, the detection mechanisms of CDs mainly refer to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching effect (SQE) and dynamic quenching effect (DQE) [56]. However, in a detection system, IFE or FRET may be found if once occurring an overlapped section between the excitation or emission spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of the quencher [79]. Generally, when DQE and FRET occur, the fluorescence lifetime will be significantly reduced, whereas IFE and SQE will not. In an effort to reveal the quenching mechanism, the UV spectra of rutin and N,S-CDs were initially investigated. The absorption band of rutin at 368 nm and 325 nm overlapped with the excitation peak of the N,S-CDs at 350 nm, indicating that the Rutin-induced fluorescence quenching can link to FRET or IFE (Fig. 7 a). To clarify how N,S-CDs quench, a steady/transient state fluorescence spectroscopy with or without rutin was deployed to investigate the alterations in fluorescence duration of N,S-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 7b, after second-order fitting calculation, the fluorescence lifetime of blank N,S-CDs was 4.54 ns, while after adding rutin, it became 4.57 ns. The fluorescence lifetime exhibited minimal alteration, thereby excluding the possibility of FRET and DQE between rutin and N,S-CDs. This suggests that quenching mechanism was likely IFE. A typical distance, usually between 10 Å and 100 Å, is necessary for FRET to function between the quencher and CDs [56]. The zeta potentials measured for N,S-CDs and Rutin in pH 7.4 PBS buffer solution were −7.58 mV and −5.46 mV, respectively, whereas that of N,S-CDs + Rutin systems was −6.47 mV (Fig. 7c). Both N,S-CDs and Rutin had negatively charged, leading to electrostatic repulsion that maintained a significant distance between them. Therefore, this further eliminated the possibility of FRET. When SQE occurs, interaction between CDs and quenchers will form a non-fluorescent ground state complex. Under this mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime has no change after appending the quencher, while the UV absorption spectrum of CDs changes [80]. For the N,S-CDs + Rutin quenching system, the appearance of novel non-fluorescent complexes could be assessed by examining the appearances of new UV absorption peaks or shift of the original absorption peaks. From Fig. 7d, it can be observed that this system did not exhibit new absorption peaks other than N,S-CDs and Rutin, nor were there any changes in the position of the peaks. The increase in absorbance was due to the superposition of absorption peaks. The increase in absorbance was due to the superposition of absorption peaks. The results showed that no new complexes generated from the N,S-CDs + Rutin system. Therefore, SQE was not identified as the primary mechanism for quenching N,S-CDs. From the experimental findings discussed earlier, it is inferred that the rutin induced the fluorescence quenching through IFE mechanisms of N,S-CDs in this system.

To further clarify the role of IFE, the Parker equation (Formula S1) was employed [81,82]. The cell geometry of the fluorescent cuvette used in the experiment is illustrated by a schematic diagram, as seen in Fig. 7e. For N,S-CDs, its fluorescence intensity and absorbance were compiled in Table 1 following the addition of varying rutin concentrations. According to CF=Fcor/Fobsd, the IFE correction factor (CF) was measured at distinct rutin concentrations.

After having added distinct rutin concentrations, the corrected fluorescence intensity (Fcor) was computed in N,S-CDs using Formula S1 and the data depicted in Fig. 7e. Based on Formula S2, the quenched efficiency (E %) was calculated using the data (Fcor and Fobsd) given in Table 1. As a correction factor, the maximum value of CF cannot exceed 3 [83]. In this experiment, the maximum value of CF is 2.95, which is less than 3, indicating that it falls within a reliable range. Furthermore, based on Formula S3, it was determined that rutin on N,S-CDs accounted for 75.86 % of the total inhibition efficiency. The results illustrated in Fig. 7f proved the hypothesis that rutin had an obvious IFE effect on the quenching. To summarize, the primary mechanism underlying rutin-induced quenching was identified as IFE in N,S-CDs.

To comprehensively estimate the fluorescent probing function of N,S-CDs in actual detection applications, this experiment investigated the influences of some common amino acids, ions and sugars on the intensity of fluorescence exhibited by N,S-CDs. The aim was to acknowledge the selectivity and anti-interference capabilities in monitoring rutin. Under the same conditions, 13 kinds of 10-fold concentration of amino acids (l-Alanine, dl-Methionine, l-Tyrosine, l-Arginine, l-Leucine, l-Isoleucine and d-Glutamic acid, etc.), 10 kinds of 5-fold concentration of metal cations (Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Mn2+ and
NH 4
+
), 6 kinds of 5-fold concentration of anions (Cl−,
HCO 3
‐
,
SO 4
2 ‐
,
NO 3
‐
,
PO 4
3 ‐
and
F ‐
), 3 kinds of 10-fold concentration of carbohydrates (Glucose, Lactose and Galactose), and 10-fold concentration of urea and uric acid were introduced to N,S-CDs, respectively. Fig. S2a and Fig. S2b showed that only rutin induces a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity in the selectivity experiment, while other interfering substances did not result in significant fluorescence quenching. In addition, in the anti-interference experiment, when rutin coexists with interfering substances, the fluorescence intensity of this system is almost insusceptible by interfering substances. This suggests that N,S-CDs under conditions containing numerous interfering substances can sensitively detect rutin. The reason may be that the absorption spectra of some interfering substances have little overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of N, S-CDs, resulting in weak IFE. The above results indicate that this experiment demonstrates excellent selectivity and resistance to interference for rutin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of rutin in human sample
ce:section.ce:para: Since this study aims to assess the suitability and practicality of the fluorescence sensor developed for detecting rutin, a generalized standard addition method has been employed for determining the rutin content in human urine and serum. Rutin was not detected in the two actual samples. Subsequently, 80, 200, and 300 μmol·L-1 rutin were added to the samples, respectively. It can be found from Table 2 that the spiked recovery of rutin in human urine was 96.0–100.6 %, with an RSD of below 1.70 % (n = 3). Similarly, the spiked recovery of rutin in human serum was 96.2–102.1 %, with an RSD of less than 2.23 % (n = 3). These findings indicate that the rutin detection method based on the fluorescence sensor was reliable, effective, fast, and convenient, demonstrating its potential for practical application in determining rutin in actual samples.

The synthesis of N,S-CDs was successfully deployed by one-step hydrothermal method employing d-aspartic acid and l-cysteine. The developed method possessed a detection range of 20–500 μmol·L-1 with a detection limit of 8.05 μmol·L-1. Furthermore, it supported the practicability of the novel technique for detecting rutin, with recoveries of 96.0–100.6 % and 96.2–102.1 % in urine and serum samples of human beings, respectively. This study provided a novel strategy for rutin detection, which broadened the idea for selecting precursor materials for the N,S-CDs preparation. According to IFE, there was no need to modify N,S-CDs or establish covalent links between an absorber and a fluorophore. Moreover, this method exhibits the superiority of quick response, a wide linear range, simplicity and eco-friendly features, and possessed the potential for further applications in medicine and pharmacy fields.

Ze Fang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Xuxin Li: Investigation, Software. Xin’an Zhang: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Wen Yan: Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision. Jianxiang Liu: Formal analysis, Software. Longshan Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.
